title: Relaxation of the Random Site Coulomb glass Model in Two Dimensions
abstract: This study investigates the influence of material density, disorder in onsite energies, and localization length on relaxation dynamics within a two-dimensional random site Coulomb glass model at half-filling. To explore relaxation laws, we calculate the eigenvalue distribution of the linear dynamical matrix using mean-field approximations. Our findings indicate that the system initially undergoes rapid relaxation through energy-lowering transitions. The depletion of the single-particle density of states (DOS) near the Fermi level leads to slow relaxation, with fluctuations diminishing according to a power law. Subsequently, the system adheres to an exponential decay law after a specific period, defined as the relaxation time, which is inversely related to the minimum eigenvalue of the dynamical matrix. As the density of the system decreases, the relaxation rate slows down, resulting in an increase in the relaxation time. For a constant density and localization length, an increase in the disorder of onsite energies results in a longer relaxation time. A significant portion of the eigenvalue spectrum remains unaffected, suggesting that a reduction in localization length concurrent with increased disorder may play an equally vital role in the slow dynamics observed.
summary: This theoretical study analyzes the relaxation dynamics of a 2D random site Coulomb glass model, showing that relaxation slows down due to the depletion of the density of states near the Fermi level. The relaxation time is found to be highly sensitive to system density and disorder strength. The work establishes key scaling laws governing the transition from initial rapid decay to long-time power-law or exponential decay.
main_problem: The study investigates the relaxation dynamics within a two-dimensional random site Coulomb glass model, focusing on how material density, onsite energy disorder, and localization length influence the relaxation process.
main_result: Slow relaxation is governed by the depletion of the single-particle density of states near the Fermi level, and the relaxation time is highly sensitive to changes in disorder and localization length.
method: The analysis uses mean-field approximations to calculate the eigenvalue distribution of the linear dynamical matrix, modeling the system's time evolution via single-particle transitions.
model_or_system: The system is the Random Site Coulomb glass (CG) model in 2D, described by a Hamiltonian involving random site energies ($\phi_i$) and Coulomb interactions. Dynamics are governed by a master equation tracking the probability of site occupations.
key_observables: Relaxation dynamics (initial rapid $ightarrow$ slow decay), eigenvalue distribution of the dynamical matrix, single-particle density of states (DOS) near the Fermi level, and the relaxation time ($	au$).
important_parameters: Material density ($n$), onsite energy disorder ($W$), and localization length ($a$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on mean-field approximations and models the dynamics assuming single-particle transitions are dominant.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the probability density of nearest neighbor distances and interaction strengths, the DOS for single-particle energies at various densities, and the distribution of the logarithm of the eigenvalues.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the relaxation process by first establishing the theoretical framework using mean-field theory, then examining the dependence of relaxation rates on varying site density, and finally discussing the roles of disorder and localization length.
why_interesting: The study provides detailed insights into slow relaxation dynamics in disordered interacting electron systems, drawing parallels between Coulomb glass physics and general disordered many-body relaxation phenomena relevant to open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
